original_abstract: Drawing inspiration from the recent synthesis of SrB3C3 and LaB3C3 sodalite-type clathrates, we employ density functional theory (DFT) to investigate the stability and superconducting properties of the XB3C3 family, including binary-guest XYB6C6 variants. Using DFT-chemical pressure analysis on members containing alkaline earth metals (X = Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba), we demonstrate that the size of the guest cation is a critical factor for driving ambient-pressure dynamical stability by optimizing the fit within the borocarbide cages. High-throughput screening of 105 binary-guest clathrates (XYB6C6) identifies 22 systems that are dynamically stable at 1 atmosphere, including 19 previously unreported phases. Our results show that the superconducting transition temperature (Tc) is highly sensitive to the average oxidation state of the guest metals; shifting the Fermi level to coincide with peaks in the density of states allows for significant tuning of Tc. Notably, KPbB6C6 is predicted to be a strong-coupling superconductor with an ambient-pressure Tc of 88 K, surpassing the boiling point of liquid nitrogen. Furthermore, we find that configurational and vibrational entropy facilitate the stabilization of these phases under high-pressure, high-temperature conditions, suggesting they can be synthesized and subsequently quenched to ambient pressure. These findings establish metallic, covalently bonded borocarbide clathrates as a robust platform for the design of conventional high-temperature superconductors.
humanized_abstract: Based on our recent work on SrB3C3 and LaB3C3 sodalite type clathrates synthesized in our lab, we explored here the stability of the recently discovered large family of superconducting clathrates denoted as XB3C3 including the additional binary-guest XYB6C6 species. We show by DFT-chemical pressure studies in the cases of some of the alkaline earth metals (X = Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba) of the former, that the size of the guest atom is an important parameter to optimize the factor of dynamic stability of these compounds at ambient pressure. High-throughput calculations of the structural stability of all possible 105 binary-guest XYB6C6 clathrates led to the prediction of dynamical stability at ambient pressure for 22 systems among which 19 are newly predicted clathrate superconductors. Here also, the high tunability of the superconducting transition temperature of these ternary borocarbide clathrates (XB3C3) was confirmed by comparing the composition for which the Fermi level matches with the DOS peaks, and also in XYB6C6 compounds with the presence of two distinct possible atomic positions for the guest atom, allowing for substantial Tc changes, respectively 57 K and 88 K for KPbB6C6 the largest in the list. Possible stabilization of the 22 XYB6C6 phases at high pressure and temperature and then their ambient pressure quenching was possible due to their large configurational and vibrational entropy. This demonstrates in addition to iron-based percolating networks, the existence of a rich family of metallic and covalent bonded compounds in which superconductivity can be obtained at high temperature.

document.output: Based on our recent work on SrB3C3 and LaB3C3 sodalite type clathrates synthesized in our lab, we explored here the stability of the recently discovered large family of superconducting clathrates denoted as XB3C3 including the additional binary-guest XYB6C6 species. We show by DFT-chemical pressure studies in the cases of some of the alkaline earth metals (X = Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba) of the former, that the size of the guest atom is an important parameter to optimize the factor of dynamic stability of these compounds at ambient pressure. High-throughput calculations of the structural stability of all possible 105 binary-guest XYB6C6 clathrates led to the prediction of dynamical stability at ambient pressure for 22 systems among which 19 are newly predicted clathrate superconductors. Here also, the high tunability of the superconducting transition temperature of these ternary borocarbide clathrates (XB3C3) was confirmed by comparing the composition for which the Fermi level matches with the DOS peaks, and also in XYB6C6 compounds with the presence of two distinct possible atomic positions for the guest atom, allowing for substantial Tc changes, respectively 57 K and 88 K for KPbB6C6 the largest in the list. Possible stabilization of the 22 XYB6C6 phases at high pressure and temperature and then their ambient pressure quenching was possible due to their large configurational and vibrational entropy. This demonstrates in addition to iron-based percolating networks, the existence of a rich family of metallic and covalent bonded compounds in which superconductivity can be obtained at high temperature.
document.input: Drawing inspiration from the recent synthesis of SrB3C3 and LaB3C3 sodalite-type clathrates, we employ density functional theory (DFT) to investigate the stability and superconducting properties of the XB3C3 family, including binary-guest XYB6C6 variants.
